sentences.text: In Plaszow, we had-- there is all over-- that was Amon Goth.
He was the main boss about the concentration camp, Plaszow.
He was, I would say, like a professional murderer.
He used to come out, people were walking, and just like this, he called them up, and he took out his revolver and he killed on the spot.
Or he used to go-- very often, he used to go with a dog, a very big one.
He didn't want to bother with the revolvers, so he just showed to the dog how to jump on this human being, and he put them in pieces on the spot.
You know, it was a terrible person.
I can't say even a person.
That was not a human being.
I haven't got words, you know, to express about this man.
So this one, he want to build a garage.
So he find out that I am a mechanic.
So he called me to the office and he says, listen-- you're going to be the mechanic.
You will take all of the garage where you will fix all the cars, what we have here.
And you will choose the people, and you will be the one responsible.
About what year was this that he called you in?
When he told me this, I couldn't say, no, I don't want to be under your hand, under your eye.
You should check me.
So I went to work to the [NON-ENGLISH]-- I mean, to the army garages, what was out of the concentration camp.
First of all, there, I didn't feel at the time in a concentration camp, because mostly they were soldiers, not SS.
But each time, you know, when I finished their engine, they took it to prove it, if it works.
And I had to take it there [INAUDIBLE] the place where they put it, you know, standing, the engine.
And we put, you know, the connections with the water, with everything.
But when I put everything together, they said, you did it the way it's supposed to be done.
So they told me.
I said, yes.
But never mind.
Whatever is going to happen with this engine, will not work proper or will breakdown, you will not work anymore.
And you will not move anymore from here.
You will be dead on the spot.
So you can imagine.
And I was-- get used to it.
But things got-- never.
Nothing happened.
They were satisfied, and the soldiers used to give me extra slice of bread.
And I knew when my friends was in ghetto, they had a daughter.
She was 2 years old.
And I knew what's going to be.
So [INAUDIBLE] friends since my childhood.
So father and mother and.
I told them, listen, tonight is going to be something terrible in the ghetto.
So I knew they have not far from my place, where I was working out of the ghetto.
So I told them you go out.
I had, you know, the news from somebody else, you know?
From a policeman from Vienna.
And I was with him, and very close, you know, in connection.
He used to tell me things what I would never believe that exist-- such police.
And he told me.
And I ask him, listen, I will take out my friend's child.
I will take on my [? bag. ?] It was such a special bag-- kind of a bag to put on.
And we put this child in it.
And after, when they left the door from the ghetto, they were free.
Nobody was-- and that was at night.
But it was very hard to go out.
So I find out when the Austrian police-- he will be on the door.
And I ask him that I want to do this.
So he told me, 10 o'clock.
You will come with those people and I let you out.
I said, I will stay in ghetto.
I just want those people to get out.
So it was the next day it happened.
That's what I was foreseeing.
That they killed the people, all of them, and especially at 2 years old.
She is today a grandmother.
She is alive.
The parents, they don't live.
I am very close.
She's living here, and she is inviting me.
You know, this child [INAUDIBLE], she knows about everything.
Because after the war, we were together, too.
So how far I was with those people, with those soldiers, that I wanted to know if the people arrived on the right place.
Because I knew what they were working.
So when I fixed up the engine and I was putting into the truck, so I ask this soldier, what he was always checking my work.
And I ask him, listen, this is ready, and I want you to go with me and to see how it works, the engine.
He said, OK.
He didn't take even a revolver with him.
He trust me.
And going with him, on the way, I say, I will ask you a favor.
Would you do me a favor?
I am here close to my friends.
You know what happened yesterday in ghetto?
And my friends, they are here with a child.
Would you be so kind just let me-- I will go there for two minutes, and I will see the people.
When I will see the people, I will be happy that they are alive because family-- I didn't have in the meantime.
So this was my family.
So the soldier said, you just go.
Joseph-- he called me-- go, and I wait for you here.
And I went into there, and I saw they are there.
They had their food and everything.
So I came back right away.
He said, you could stay other few minutes.
And we went back to work, to where was the garages from the army.
And that was the way of my work.
And the other people-- because it was, I would say, half and half-- half men and half women.
The woman used to do the work to clean up the soldiers, the officers-- they had the rooms.
They used to live there.
And the men supposed all of them to be the mechanics, but we were only three of us.
And all others, because of us-- so I took them today, I took two, three others to help me, that I need them, [? they ?] [? are ?] very important.
It was a risk of life if they would find out, if they would check me.
But they know for the work I am responsible.
And those people survived because of me.
And I used to sign every day, how many goes to work and how many comes back.
So one day, Amon Goth-- you know, the murderer.
Our boss from the Plaszow.
Every day before we went to work, it was [? appel. ?] They were talking to us.
In the meantime, they killed a few people before we went to work.
So one day, we came back, and a boy with his sister-- they run away.
That was the day after when he said, if one will be missing, he will kill all the people that are coming back.
And it happened to me.
And I came back and I said, it's missing two.
I can-- nothing I can do.
They run away, and I don't know when because I was working.
As usual, I work, and I'm responsible for my work.
So I knew that he was going to kill me.
But usually, if he wants to kill-- so he used to say, don't let them in.
We have to wait, you know, on the door when we arrived.
And then he [INAUDIBLE] in himself, you know the SS.
They took the whole group, and they shot them down.
It was a special place for it.
And the name was [GERMAN].
This was very famous.
If you want, you can mark down.
They were killing the people.
When he said that we are going to let us free, not to take us there, I felt that's not so bad.
That they don't kill us right now.
But everybody was shivering.
We didn't know who was going to be dead. 12 o'clock at night, he called me.
He sent-- the Jewish police was also in the camp.
And two Jewish police, they took me to of the office, where he was at night.
You know, he was there.
He find out that people want to run away, so it was the place where he used to kill them.
And he used to have such a helpers from [INAUDIBLE], also murderers from Ukraine.
Anti-Semites-- they were before the war, so that was very good for him.
So when I saw people was hanging, and they were shooting there, so I knew where I came.
But I was lucky enough because one of the Jewish police, the officer, was my friend.
We used to go to school together.
When he saw me, I just enter, and the police [INAUDIBLE] to him that I am here.
So I told them in Polish, listen, I beg you, I know for what they called me here, but you're the only one.
Maybe you could do something for me.
I shall stay alive.
So he says, Joe, I do for you whatever it would be possible.
In the meantime, this [INAUDIBLE], he turned from there, and he's coming where I was standing by the door.
He is coming, and I had to tell him what happened, that they run away.
Oh, so you are the one.
So he took out the revolver.

When he took out the revolver, my friend, this chief of the police, he says, listen sir, we have-- this is the only mechanic what we have here in garage, and he is the one what we call him always on night when it happens something with your car.
So then he turned the revolver on other side, and he give me just a [INAUDIBLE] in my head.
When he turned the revolver-- so the chief of the Jewish police, he told me right away, he will not kill you if you turn his gun this way.
So he will stay alive.
So when he mentioned to him this is the only one, so he told me, oh, I remember you.
You came here as a mechanic, and you're supposed to be in the garage, to work in the garage.
I said, listen sir, you didn't tell me that I have to go to the garage.
I didn't want to go to the garage, but I just tell him, and he really didn't tell me.
And when he need it at night, the garage was closed, so they knew where I lived, in which barrack, [INAUDIBLE] number.
I remember it today.
So when I fixed the carr-- his car, with other friend he knew also.
Very good.
He was the good one.
So we went.
But he says, now I want to see you should drive the car.
It was a Volkswagen.
Very cheap one, you know?
But always he had problems with it.
So I went with my friend.
He said, go there on the hill up and down and try me the brakes and everything.
And everything was fine.
So he gave me a piece of paper written to the kitchen that we both have to get meat for the whole week to eat.
Every day we had full of meat and the soup.
It happened only once that we were the one.
So that's why he didn't kill me.
But he told me, you will not go anymore there to work.
I need you here.
I said, sir, you have to talk with the officers there.
They are working for the front/ we prepare the trucks for the front-- what they're sending out.
I am the one-- they are coming, they pick me up, I have to go.
If you will not let me go, sure, I will stay here.
He says, I will fix it up.
So he let me for a few days, and then he stop the whole place not to go anymore there because they run away and this and that.
But I start in the garage.
And in the garage was a friend of mine also.
He was a good mechanic.
In the meantime, they needed a good mechanic special to diesel machines because there were [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is a [NON-ENGLISH]?
There were [? hocking-- ?] that was hills what was stones inside.
And they had to work all day to give the stones this [GERMAN].
He shouldn't go to the front because he was delivering, he was working.
And he had to look the machines-- they shall work.
So when I came to him, he asked for me, this one, and he went to get and give him the permission.
He said, you are here.
And I know that you are a good mechanic.
But if you will not do the job, what's supposed to be done here, you would be killed in the [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was specialized in this also.
Because on his own, he liked to go-- whenever it was they took Jews for killing, he used to go.
They didn't ask him, but he likes it.
And he got two machines.
They went very bad condition.
And I changed it.
Instead of solar or gas, I put it on electric.
I changed it.
And I took it to the garage there.
Everybody knew me in the garage.
And I did it, the job.
So he said, when is it going to be ready?
I said, in two days.
And I came with those machines.
The machines were working.
He used to go-- he used to bring us bread.
He use to bring us even parcels, what was not for Jews because shoes wasn't allowed.
It was Polish people also there in the concentration camp.
They were working, you know, political.
They were against the Germans, so they put them there in the camps.
And this one, he said, you-- because you're working like this, and you fix the machines, you will go to Brnenec.
I make for you that you got the permission to go.
And the owner of Brnenec to go was Schindler.
And the next two days, he called up Schindler.
Schindler was a [INAUDIBLE].
No, he was not a soldier, an SS officer.
And he came, and he told me right away, he told them before what I am doing.
He says, you belong to me.
Next month or in three, four weeks, goes transportation to Brnenec.
And you take all the machines.
I make you, right now, responsible for the machines.
And you can put in whatever you have here, some spares, some [INAUDIBLE] or whatever, because the machines they go straight to Sudetenland.
Brnenec.
That was already 1944.
When he told me this-- and he told me [NON-ENGLISH], not [NON-ENGLISH], like they used to say in German and Polish-- [INAUDIBLE].
So he said me, like we say here, you.
So we say to everybody.
But that was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was a very, very nice man.
And he promised me right away that I shall be the one, and there he got also the machines [INAUDIBLE].
And that was the way what I finished in Plaszow.
I can't go through all the details because in Plaszow, was killings day by day, thousands of human beings.
You know, it's very hard to talk about it, but I am telling you how I felt.
I was running from place to place, and I had the right to go because I was going-- always something I had to fix.
So that's why he didn't kill me.
He knew that the work what I am doing.
You can imagine.
Like I had mentioned to you, he used to give me meat for the whole week because I was working.
So then they started to liquidate Plaszow.
So also, they killed the people-- whoever was not able even-- who was a little bit stronger.
They were prepared tracks, and they send them to Auschwitz.
About what year was that when they started liquidating Plaszow?
In '43, we had Dr.
Mengele.
You know, you hear about Dr.
Mengele.
He made the segregation.
And that time, I was sick.
I didn't feel good.
I had [NON-ENGLISH] on my legs all over.
Here and here.
So I knew that when I go there, he will see it, [? he will hurt ?] me.
So I put on the big shoes-- [? they're ?] [? hard. ?] And I took my belongings.
And he looked on my face.
He saw-- on the face, I looked very good.
So he told me, right.
So I was also-- my life wasn't worth that much.
I have to say, it's a miracle that I am alive, you see?
But I was working a lot with my head, you know, to help myself.
Nobody else could help me.
So that was the time what was after the segregation, so I was already the healthy one.
And I was still working other few weeks, and then they decide to go-- to move to Brnenec, to Schindler.
But they sent us through Gross-Rosen.
It was only a very bad concentration camp.
But we were there only for a few days.
We didn't know in the beginning.
We were sure that they're going to kill us.
But this was only the transport-- what we're supposed to go to Schindler.
We were a thousand men and 300 women at Schindler's, what we survived.
And only one girl passed away because she was sick.
She had the sickness.
No one more.
He was looking after us.
This is not now-- for now, I will tell you.
He had other factory closer to Plaszow on the other side from Plaszow.
Nobody was killed there.
So he had there some 600 men, and 400 was from Plaszow.
And the women also.
They were working there. 200, and 100 were in Plaszow.
But they sent them through Auschwitz, and we went through Gross-Rosen.
And Auschwitz was, you know, very dangerous, and took him quite a few days.
They didn't send the people, so he came back from Brnenec.
We were already in Brnenec.
And he came back to pick up the women, because it was a few days they didn't send them.
And they were elderly women, too.
So he said they are specialized.
They ask him, what for you need those?
They don't work.
He said, I need them.
They are specialized.
I learn them to do the work what's supposed to be done.
And then all of them, they send it to Brnenec.
No, because we were only in Gross-Rosen.
They kept us for five days.
It was seven days.
They used to hit us there.
They didn't give us food.
We were hungry.
And one day, we're supposed to go to Brnenec already.
So they called us, you know, to cut the hair, and they showed us to take a shower before we go.
We didn't know.
We knew that this is not a shower.
That they send us to burn down.
But it was not true.
The first few people, what they called, nobody wants to go.
So they, by force, they push a few.
So then we go, all of us.
It was really a shower.
And from there, it was one day more.
We were ready, and we went by train to Brnenec.
So that was the transportation, what we had from Plaszow to Brnenec.
